---
title: "Capacitors, inductors, and transients"
description: "Derive stored-energy element relations, predict first-order RC and RL transients, and make bounded switching and discharge decisions."
chapter-id: F07
status: draft
---

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter maturity — draft"}
This chapter provides a complete instructional path: constitutive relations and
stored-energy derivations, initial-condition reasoning, RC and RL step responses,
an executable cross-check, explicitly synthetic diagnostic data, switching
protection, non-ideal behavior, a guarded discharge design, and a full exercise
set. It remains subject to technical and editorial revision. Physical evidence
belongs in aligned Lab **L05**; calculations and synthetic data here do not
qualify hardware. See the [reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd) for status meanings.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary for stored-energy work"}
Practical work discussed here is limited to **current-limited,
extra-low-voltage** sources, low stored energy, approved components, local
supervision, and an approved procedure. A disconnected capacitor may remain
charged, and an interrupted inductor current may create a voltage far above the
supply. De-energize before changing topology; discharge through an approved
resistor; then prove the voltage is safe with a suitable instrument. Never short
a charged capacitor with a tool and never interrupt an unknown inductive load.
This chapter is not authorization for mains, high voltage, high-energy capacitor
banks, battery packs, ignition systems, motors, medical equipment, or
safety-critical systems [@iec61010].
:::

## Central question

> How do circuits remember energy and respond when conditions change?

Close a switch that connects a resistor and capacitor to a DC source. The
capacitor voltage does not jump immediately to the source voltage; it approaches
it. Replace the capacitor by an inductor. Now the branch current, rather than the
element voltage, approaches its final value. Open the switch again and both
elements can continue affecting the circuit after the source connection has
changed. The schematic has changed instantaneously, but an energy-storing state
has not.

Before calculating, predict the following for the circuits in this chapter.

- Which quantity cannot change abruptly when all currents and voltages remain
  finite: capacitor voltage or capacitor current?
- Which quantity cannot change abruptly for an inductor?
- Does a larger series resistance make an RC capacitor charge faster or slower?
- Does the same resistance make an RL normalized response settle faster or
  slower, and does it change the initial current slope?
- At five time constants, is the response exactly finished?
- When a relay coil is switched off, where can its stored energy go?

The answers follow from charge conservation, Faraday's law, and energy balance;
they are not separate rules to memorize. The same physical circuit will be
described as a schematic, a differential equation, an exponential waveform, an
energy transfer, and a measurement. Agreement among those descriptions is the
evidence that the reasoning is sound [@sedra2020microelectronic;
@horowitz2015art].

## Learning outcomes

This chapter assumes [F04](f04-networks-kirchhoff.qmd): KCL, KVL, and Thévenin
reduction; [F05](f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd): passive-sign power and energy
balances; and [M02](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd):
derivatives, integrals, and first-order differential equations. After completing
it, you should be able to:

- define capacitance and inductance with declared voltage, current, flux, and
  winding references, and derive their ideal terminal relations and stored
  energies;
- explain exactly why ideal capacitor voltage and ideal inductor current are
  continuous when excitation remains finite, and identify the impulse required
  to violate that continuity;
- reduce a one-storage-element linear network to its Thévenin resistance, write
  and solve its first-order differential equation, and interpret its natural,
  forced, complete, zero-input, and zero-state responses;
- predict RC and RL transients from the initial value, final value, and time
  constant, including signs, units, limiting cases, and component tolerances;
- trace energy during charging, discharge, and switch opening without counting
  stored energy, dissipation, and outward heat transfer twice;
- diagnose departures caused by leakage, ESR, winding resistance, saturation,
  parasitic capacitance, source resistance, or instrument loading; and
- select a discharge or flyback path and state a bounded acceptance rule with
  operating conditions, uncertainty, loading, and margin.

## Electric and magnetic storage variables

Resistance relates voltage and current at an instant. An ideal capacitor or
inductor relates a terminal quantity to the **rate of change** of an internal
state. That state is what gives a first-order circuit memory.

| Element | Stored state | Linear constitutive relation | Terminal relation | Energy for zero reference state |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| capacitor | charge separation $q$ | $q=Cv_C$ | $i_C=C\,\mathrm dv_C/\mathrm dt$ | $W_C=\tfrac12 Cv_C^2$ |
| inductor | flux linkage $\lambda$ | $\lambda=Li_L$ | $v_L=L\,\mathrm di_L/\mathrm dt$ | $W_L=\tfrac12 Li_L^2$ |

These are ideal, lumped, linear, time-invariant relations. $C$ and $L$ are
constant in the stated operating range; fields are assumed confined well enough
that one two-terminal element represents them; and loss, leakage, coupling,
saturation, and parasitics are initially excluded. Real components approximate
these relations only over bounded voltage, current, temperature, and frequency
ranges [@sedra2020microelectronic; @ulaby2015electromagnetics].

Charge separation $q$ and flux linkage $\lambda$ are the physical storage
variables in this description. Because @eq-f07-qcv and $\lambda=Li_L$ are
one-to-one when $C$ and $L$ are positive constants, $v_C$ and $i_L$ are
convenient state coordinates in the ideal linear range. That convenience must
not be extended unchanged into nonlinear or hysteretic operation.

### Capacitance, current, and stored electric energy

Name the capacitor terminals A and B. Define
$v_C(t)\equiv V_A(t)-V_B(t)$, marked $+$ at A and $-$ at B. Define $i_C(t)$ as
positive entering terminal A. These are passive-sign references. Let $q(t)$ be
the free charge associated with terminal A relative to the chosen zero-charge
state. For a linear capacitor, **capacitance is the proportionality**

$$
q(t)=C v_C(t),
$$ {#eq-f07-qcv}

where $C>0$ has unit farad:
$1~\text{F}=1~\text{C}/\text{V}$. For parallel plates of area $A$, separation
$d$, and approximately uniform dielectric permittivity $\epsilon$, neglecting
fringing gives $C\approx\epsilon A/d$. This geometric expression is an
approximation; @eq-f07-qcv is the terminal constitutive relation used here
[@griffiths2017electrodynamics].

Current is the rate at which terminal charge changes. Because the current
reference enters the voltage-positive terminal,

$$
\boxed{i_C(t)=\frac{\mathrm dq}{\mathrm dt}
=C\frac{\mathrm dv_C(t)}{\mathrm dt}.}
$$ {#eq-f07-capacitor-iv}

Equation @eq-f07-capacitor-iv is exact for the stated ideal constant-$C$
description. Its dimensions teach the relation:

$$
[C\,\mathrm dv/\mathrm dt]
=\frac{\text{C}}{\text{V}}\frac{\text{V}}{\text{s}}
=\frac{\text{C}}{\text{s}}=\text{A}.
$$

A constant capacitor voltage implies zero ideal branch current, but it does not
imply zero stored energy. Conversely, a finite constant current creates a linear
voltage ramp with slope $i_C/C$. Reversing only the current reference changes
the signed equation to $i'_C=-C\,\mathrm dv_C/\mathrm dt$; the physical device
does not change.

With passive-sign references, terminal power absorbed is
$p_C=v_C i_C$. Starting from $v_C=0$ and integrating,

$$
W_C(v_C)
=\int v_C i_C\,\mathrm dt
=\int_0^{v_C} v\,C\,\mathrm dv
=\boxed{\frac12 C v_C^2}.
$$ {#eq-f07-capacitor-energy}

The unit check is
$\text{F}\,\text{V}^2=(\text{C}/\text{V})\text{V}^2
=\text{C}\,\text{V}=\text{J}$. Energy depends on $v_C^2$, so reversing voltage
polarity does not make stored energy negative. The sign of $p_C$, however, says
whether energy is entering or leaving at that instant.

### Inductance, voltage, and stored magnetic energy

Choose a winding direction and a positive surface normal through each turn by
the right-hand rule. Let $\Phi_B$ be magnetic flux through that oriented surface
and let the signed flux linkage be $\lambda=N\Phi_B$ for $N$ identical linked
turns. Mark inductor terminal A positive relative to B and define $i_L$ entering
A. Choose the winding sense so positive $i_L$ produces positive $\lambda$.
Faraday's law then gives the passive-sign terminal relation

$$
v_L(t)=\frac{\mathrm d\lambda(t)}{\mathrm dt}.
$$ {#eq-f07-faraday-terminal}

The minus sign in the field-law form is contained in the declared terminal,
winding, path, and surface orientations; it must not be added again. For a
linear, uncoupled inductor, **inductance is the proportionality**

$$
\lambda(t)=L i_L(t),
$$

so constant $L$ gives

$$
\boxed{v_L(t)=L\frac{\mathrm di_L(t)}{\mathrm dt}.}
$$ {#eq-f07-inductor-iv}

The henry satisfies

$$
[L]=\frac{\text{V}\,\text{s}}{\text{A}},
\qquad
[L\,\mathrm di/\mathrm dt]
=\frac{\text{V}\,\text{s}}{\text{A}}
  \frac{\text{A}}{\text{s}}=\text{V}.
$$

A constant ideal inductor current therefore requires zero inductor voltage but
may correspond to nonzero stored energy. A constant finite voltage produces a
linear current ramp. Integrating passive-sign power from zero current gives

$$
W_L(i_L)
=\int v_L i_L\,\mathrm dt
=\int_0^{i_L} L i\,\mathrm di
=\boxed{\frac12 L i_L^2}.
$$ {#eq-f07-inductor-energy}

Its unit is
$\text{H}\,\text{A}^2=(\text{V}\,\text{s}/\text{A})\text{A}^2
=\text{V}\,\text{A}\,\text{s}=\text{J}$. The formula is not valid with a
single constant $L$ once a magnetic core becomes appreciably nonlinear or
saturates; then $\lambda(i)$ must be used and energy requires the actual
magnetization relation,
$W=\int i\,\mathrm d\lambda$. The distinct integral
$\int\lambda\,\mathrm di$ is magnetic coenergy; the two are equal only for the
linear relation used in @eq-f07-inductor-energy
[@ulaby2015electromagnetics].

### Series and parallel combinations

Network reduction remains possible when the elements are ideal and uncoupled.
The conditions matter.

| Connection | Equivalent value | Required condition |
|---|---:|---|
| capacitors in parallel | $C_\text{eq}=\sum C_k$ | same terminal voltage |
| capacitors in series | $C_\text{eq}^{-1}=\sum C_k^{-1}$ | intermediate nodes initially carry no unaccounted net free charge |
| inductors in series | $L_\text{eq}=\sum L_k$ | negligible mutual coupling |
| inductors in parallel | $L_\text{eq}^{-1}=\sum L_k^{-1}$ | negligible coupling and compatible initial currents |

Nearby inductors can share flux, adding mutual-inductance terms; series
capacitors can divide voltage unevenly through leakage and tolerance. The simple
table is a terminal reduction, not a universal construction rule
[@sedra2020microelectronic].

## Continuity follows bounded excitation

Continuity is a consequence of the constitutive relations, not a claim that a
state can never change quickly.

Integrate @eq-f07-capacitor-iv over a switching interval from $0^-$ to $0^+$:

$$
v_C(0^+)-v_C(0^-)
=\frac{1}{C}\int_{0^-}^{0^+} i_C(t)\,\mathrm dt.
$$ {#eq-f07-cap-continuity}

If $i_C$ is bounded in a neighborhood of the switching instant and contains no
impulse, the integral tends to zero with the interval and

$$
\boxed{v_C(0^+)=v_C(0^-).}
$$ {#eq-f07-cap-initial}

An ideal voltage step across an ideal capacitor is possible only with a current
impulse whose area is $C\Delta v_C$. Real source resistance, lead inductance,
ESR, and finite switching time bound the current and spread the change over time.

Likewise, integrate @eq-f07-inductor-iv:

$$
i_L(0^+)-i_L(0^-)
=\frac{1}{L}\int_{0^-}^{0^+} v_L(t)\,\mathrm dt.
$$ {#eq-f07-ind-continuity}

If $v_L$ is bounded near the switching instant and contains no impulse, then

$$
\boxed{i_L(0^+)=i_L(0^-).}
$$ {#eq-f07-ind-initial}

An ideal current step through an ideal inductor requires a voltage impulse of
area $L\Delta i_L$. Opening a mechanical switch tries to force the branch current
to zero while parasitic capacitance is very small; the inductor responds with
whatever voltage the surrounding insulation, switch, arc, or clamp allows. That
is why “the inductor resists current change” is useful only after the energy path
and voltage limit are named.

::: {.callout-important title="What is continuous, and what may jump"}
A capacitor's **voltage** is continuous under finite current; its current may
jump when the applied network changes. An inductor's **current** is continuous
under finite voltage; its voltage may jump. “A capacitor is a short at turn-on”
and “an inductor is an open at turn-on” are unsafe slogans: they are true only
for particular zero initial conditions and particular surrounding networks.
:::

## First-order RC response

@fig-f07-rc keeps the same $v_C$ polarity and downward $i_C$ reference in both
configurations. During discharge the physical current flows opposite the arrow,
so $i_C<0$; the reference does not rotate merely to keep a result positive.

![A source-driven RC charging circuit and the corresponding source-free discharge circuit. The capacitor reference polarity and current arrow are unchanged across switching.](../../../build/circuits/f07-rc-transient.png){#fig-f07-rc fig-alt="Panel a shows voltage source V_S connected through switch S and Thevenin resistance R_th to capacitor C. Panel b shows the source removed and R_th connected across the capacitor. TP1 is the capacitor-positive node relative to TP0. Current i_C is referenced downward into the capacitor-positive terminal in both panels."}

### Governing differential equation

After the switch changes at $t=0$, replace the rest of any linear resistive
network seen by the capacitor with its Thévenin voltage $V_\text{th}$ and
resistance $R_\text{th}>0$. Independent sources are deactivated only when finding
$R_\text{th}$; dependent sources, if present, remain and require a test source as
in F04. With $v_C$ positive at TP1, KVL and
@eq-f07-capacitor-iv give

$$
V_\text{th}=R_\text{th}i_C+v_C
=R_\text{th}C\frac{\mathrm dv_C}{\mathrm dt}+v_C.
$$ {#eq-f07-rc-ode}

This is an exact first-order equation for constant $V_\text{th}$, constant
$R_\text{th}$, and constant $C$ in the ideal lumped description. Rearranging,

$$
\frac{\mathrm dv_C}{\mathrm dt}
=-\frac{1}{R_\text{th}C}(v_C-V_\text{th}).
$$

The derivative is proportional to the distance from the final value. Define
$y(t)=v_C(t)-V_\text{th}$ and $\tau_C=R_\text{th}C$. When $y\ne0$, separation
of variables from $0^+$ to $t$ gives

$$
\frac{\mathrm dy}{y}=-\frac{\mathrm dt}{\tau_C},
\qquad
\int_{y(0^+)}^{y(t)}\frac{\mathrm dy'}{y'}
=-\int_0^t\frac{\mathrm dt'}{\tau_C},
$$

and therefore

$$
\ln\left|\frac{y(t)}{y(0^+)}\right|=-\frac{t}{\tau_C},
\qquad
y(t)=y(0^+)e^{-t/\tau_C}.
$$

The sign of $y$ cannot change in this homogeneous equation, which removes the
absolute-value ambiguity. If $y(0^+)=0$, the identically zero solution follows
directly: the capacitor already has its final voltage. Applying
$v_C(0^+)=V_0$ in either case yields

$$
\boxed{v_C(t)=V_\infty+
  \bigl(V_0-V_\infty\bigr)e^{-t/\tau_C},\qquad t\ge0,}
$$ {#eq-f07-rc-complete}

where

$$
V_\infty=V_\text{th},
\qquad
\boxed{\tau_C=R_\text{th}C.}
$$ {#eq-f07-rc-tau}

The time constant has units
$\Omega\,\text{F}=(\text{V}/\text{A})(\text{C}/\text{V})
=\text{C}/\text{A}=\text{s}$. It is not “the time to finish.” At $t=\tau_C$,
the distance from final is $e^{-1}\approx0.368$ of its initial value, so 63.2%
of a rising change is complete.

The term $V_\infty$ is the **forced** or steady response to the post-switch
source. The exponential is the **natural** response. Their sum is the complete
response. With $V_0=0$, it is also called the zero-state response. With
$V_\text{th}=0$ and $V_0\ne0$, it is the zero-input response. These labels refer
to the mathematical decomposition, not to separate voltages physically stored
in different places [@oppenheim1996signals].

### Worked example: a 5 V charging transient

Let an ideal 5.00 V DC step drive $R_\text{th}=10.0~\text{k}\Omega$ and
$C=10.0~\mu\text{F}$, initially discharged. The estimate is a response on the
scale of $10^4\times10^{-5}=10^{-1}$ s. Exactly within the ideal description,

$$
\tau_C=(10.0\times10^3~\Omega)(10.0\times10^{-6}~\text{F})
=0.100~\text{s}.
$$

The initial and final values are $V_0=0$ and $V_\infty=5.00$ V, hence

$$
v_C(t)=5.00~\text{V}\left(1-e^{-t/(0.100~\text{s})}\right).
$$

At one time constant,

$$
v_C(0.100~\text{s})
=5.00(1-e^{-1})~\text{V}=3.16~\text{V}.
$$

The associated current is

$$
i_C(t)=\frac{5.00~\text{V}}{10.0~\text{k}\Omega}
e^{-t/(0.100~\text{s})}
=(0.500~\text{mA})e^{-t/(0.100~\text{s})}.
$$

It starts at $+0.500$ mA and decays to zero. If the capacitor instead begins at
7.00 V while the post-switch source remains 5.00 V, then
$V_0-V_\infty=+2.00$ V: the voltage decays toward 5.00 V and $i_C<0$. The same
equation handles charging and discharging; the sign comes from declared
references and boundary values.

### Worked example: a switched divider reduced at the capacitor port

The preceding circuit was already in Thévenin form. @fig-f07-thevenin requires
the full F04-to-F07 procedure. Assume switch $S$ has been open for long enough
before $t=0$ that $R_2$ has discharged the capacitor. The switch closes at
$t=0$ and the ideal 9.00 V DC source remains constant.

![A switched resistor divider driving a capacitor at TP1.](../../../build/circuits/f07-thevenin-transient.png){#fig-f07-thevenin fig-alt="A 9 volt source and closing switch feed a 2 kilohm resistor R1 into TP1. A 4 kilohm resistor R2 and 15 microfarad capacitor C both connect from TP1 to reference TP0. Capacitor voltage is positive at TP1 and current i_C is referenced downward."}

**Initial state.** With $S$ open, $R_2$ connects TP1 to TP0. At pre-switch DC
steady state, $v_C(0^-)=0$ V. No current impulse is present, so continuity gives
$v_C(0^+)=0$ V.

**Post-switch final value.** At $t\to\infty$, the ideal capacitor current tends
to zero. The remaining divider gives

$$
V_\infty=V_\text{th}
=(9.00~\text{V})\frac{4.00~\text{k}\Omega}
{2.00~\text{k}\Omega+4.00~\text{k}\Omega}
=6.00~\text{V}.
$$

**Post-switch resistance.** Deactivate the independent ideal voltage source by
replacing it with a short while retaining the closed-switch topology. Looking
from TP1 to TP0 then gives

$$
R_\text{th}=R_1\parallel R_2
=2.00~\text{k}\Omega\parallel4.00~\text{k}\Omega
=1.333~\text{k}\Omega.
$$

Thus

$$
\tau_C=R_\text{th}C
=(1.333~\text{k}\Omega)(15.0~\mu\text{F})
=20.0~\text{ms},
$$

and the complete prediction is

$$
\boxed{v_C(t)=6.00~\text{V}
\left(1-e^{-t/(20.0~\text{ms})}\right),\qquad t\ge0.}
$$

At one time constant, $v_C=3.79$ V. The predicted initial slope is

$$
\left.\frac{\mathrm dv_C}{\mathrm dt}\right|_{0^+}
=\frac{V_\infty-V_0}{\tau_C}
=300~\text{V/s},
$$

so $i_C(0^+)=C(300~\text{V/s})=4.50$ mA. Direct network analysis at
$t=0^+$ agrees: TP1 is held initially at 0 V by the capacitor state, giving
$(9.00~\text{V})/(2.00~\text{k}\Omega)=4.50$ mA through $R_1$ and zero through
$R_2$. At final DC, the capacitor current is zero and both resistor currents are
1.50 mA. Initial condition, final divider, Thévenin reduction, slope, and KCL
therefore tell one consistent story.

### Initial and final value procedure

For any one-capacitor, first-order linear circuit:

1. Solve the $t<0$ circuit far enough before switching to obtain $v_C(0^-)$.
2. Apply continuity: $v_C(0^+)=v_C(0^-)$ unless an explicitly represented
   current impulse exists.
3. Solve the post-switch DC circuit for $v_C(\infty)$; only at this steady state
   does an ideal capacitor branch carry zero current and behave as an open.
4. Find $R_\text{th}$ seen by the capacitor in the **post-switch topology**.
5. Form $\tau_C=R_\text{th}C$ and substitute the three values into @eq-f07-rc-complete.
6. Check $t=0$, $t\to\infty$, the initial slope, units, and energy direction.

If two independent capacitor voltages remain after reduction, the circuit is
generally second order and one time constant is insufficient.

## First-order RL response

@fig-f07-rl retains the downward $i_L$ reference when the source is removed. The
natural-response loop then produces $v_L<0$: the inductor delivers stored energy
while current continues downward and decays.

![A source-driven RL circuit and the corresponding source-free loop. The current reference remains downward through the inductor in both configurations.](../../../build/circuits/f07-rl-transient.png){#fig-f07-rl fig-alt="Panel a shows source V_S and switch S driving resistance R_th and inductor L in series. Panel b shows R_th and L in a source-free closed loop. Current i_L is referenced downward through L in both panels; TP1 and TP0 expose the inductor terminals."}

### Governing differential equation

Replace the linear network seen by the inductor after switching with a Thévenin
source $V_\text{th}$ and the **total** series resistance $R_\text{th}>0$,
including source resistance and winding resistance included in the first-order
description. KVL with the references in @fig-f07-rl gives

$$
V_\text{th}=R_\text{th}i_L+v_L
=R_\text{th}i_L+L\frac{\mathrm di_L}{\mathrm dt}.
$$ {#eq-f07-rl-ode}

Subtract $I_\infty=V_\text{th}/R_\text{th}$ and define
$z(t)=i_L(t)-I_\infty$. Then
$\mathrm dz/\mathrm dt=-z/(L/R_\text{th})$, exactly the equation just solved
for $y$. Applying $i_L(0^+)=I_0$ gives

$$
\boxed{i_L(t)=I_\infty+
  \bigl(I_0-I_\infty\bigr)e^{-t/\tau_L},\qquad t\ge0,}
$$ {#eq-f07-rl-complete}

with

$$
I_\infty=\frac{V_\text{th}}{R_\text{th}},
\qquad
\boxed{\tau_L=\frac{L}{R_\text{th}}.}
$$ {#eq-f07-rl-tau}

The unit check is
$\text{H}/\Omega=(\text{V}\,\text{s}/\text{A})/(\text{V}/\text{A})
=\text{s}$. Increasing resistance slows an RC normalized response because
$\tau_C=RC$, but reduces an RL time constant because $\tau_L=L/R$. Thus an RL
natural decay, or its normalized distance from final, settles faster. For a fixed
ideal voltage step, increasing $R$ also lowers $I_\infty=V/R$ while leaving the
initial slope $\mathrm di/\mathrm dt|_{0^+}=V/L$ unchanged. “The current rises
faster” is therefore incomplete unless the compared quantity is named.

### Worked example: current buildup and voltage reversal

Let an ideal 5.00 V DC step drive $R_\text{th}=100~\Omega$ and
$L=100~\text{mH}$ from $I_0=0$. The final current and time constant are

$$
I_\infty=\frac{5.00~\text{V}}{100~\Omega}=50.0~\text{mA},
\qquad
\tau_L=\frac{0.100~\text{H}}{100~\Omega}=1.00~\text{ms}.
$$

Therefore

$$
i_L(t)=50.0~\text{mA}\left(1-e^{-t/(1.00~\text{ms})}\right),
$$

and at $t=1.00$ ms the current is 31.6 mA. From KVL,

$$
v_L(t)=5.00~\text{V}\,e^{-t/(1.00~\text{ms})}.
$$

The inductor initially takes the full +5.00 V while the resistor drop is zero;
at steady DC the inductor voltage is zero and the resistor takes the full source
voltage.

Now remove the source while preserving a 100 $\Omega$ closed discharge loop.
Continuity gives $i_L(0^+)=50.0$ mA and $I_\infty=0$, so

$$
i_L(t)=50.0~\text{mA}\,e^{-t/(1.00~\text{ms})}.
$$

The current retains its positive downward direction, but

$$
v_L(0^+)=L\left.\frac{\mathrm di_L}{\mathrm dt}\right|_{0^+}
=-(100~\Omega)(50.0~\text{mA})=-5.00~\text{V}.
$$

The voltage reverses so the inductor can deliver energy into the resistor. A
negative passive-sign power $v_L i_L$ confirms delivery rather than absorption.

### Initial and final value procedure

The corresponding one-inductor procedure is:

1. Find $i_L(0^-)$ from the pre-switch circuit.
2. Apply $i_L(0^+)=i_L(0^-)$ unless a voltage impulse is explicitly present.
3. Find $i_L(\infty)$ from the post-switch DC circuit; only then may an ideal
   inductor be replaced by a zero-voltage short.
4. Find the total resistance seen in the post-switch current path.
5. Form $\tau_L=L/R_\text{th}$ and use @eq-f07-rl-complete.
6. Check the initial inductor voltage, both endpoints, dimensions, and the path
   that absorbs the initial magnetic energy.

An ideal inductor directly across an ideal voltage source has
$R_\text{th}=0$ and no finite DC final current; current ramps rather than
approaching an exponential steady state. That limiting case is outside the
$R_\text{th}>0$ solution above, not a division-by-zero nuisance to ignore.

## Unified first-order response and settling

Both circuits are instances of the linear state equation

$$
\frac{\mathrm dx}{\mathrm dt}
=-\frac{1}{\tau}(x-x_\infty),
$$ {#eq-f07-first-order-state}

whose complete solution is

$$
\boxed{x(t)=x_\infty+
\bigl(x(0^+)-x_\infty\bigr)e^{-t/\tau}.}
$$ {#eq-f07-first-order-solution}

For RC, $x=v_C$ and $\tau=R_\text{th}C$; for RL, $x=i_L$ and
$\tau=L/R_\text{th}$. The normalized remaining error is

$$
\frac{x(t)-x_\infty}{x(0^+)-x_\infty}=e^{-t/\tau}.
$$

The duality is useful only when its differences remain visible:

| Feature | One-capacitor RC | One-inductor RL |
|---|---|---|
| convenient linear state coordinate | $v_C$ | $i_L$ |
| continuous under bounded, impulse-free excitation | $v_C$ | $i_L$ |
| post-switch DC replacement for final value | open capacitor branch | zero-voltage inductor branch |
| time constant | $R_\text{th}C$ | $L/R_\text{th}$ |
| initial derivative | $(V_\infty-V_0)/\tau_C$ | $(I_\infty-I_0)/\tau_L$ |
| effect of increasing $R_\text{th}$ | normalized settling slows | normalized settling speeds up |

This makes useful settling screens independent of amplitude:

| Elapsed time | Remaining distance | Completed fraction of a monotonic rise |
|---:|---:|---:|
| $1\tau$ | 36.8% | 63.2% |
| $2\tau$ | 13.5% | 86.5% |
| $3\tau$ | 4.98% | 95.0% |
| $4.61\tau$ | 1.00% | 99.0% |
| $5\tau$ | 0.674% | 99.326% |

The exponential never becomes mathematically zero at finite time. “Settled” is
therefore a requirement, not a natural constant: name the permitted error band,
noise, measurement uncertainty, and duration. For a fractional remaining error
$\epsilon$ with $0<\epsilon<1$,

$$
t_\text{settle}=-\tau\ln\epsilon.
$$ {#eq-f07-settling}

## Energy balance during transients

Choose a boundary enclosing the ideal resistor and capacitor in the charging
circuit, but not the source. Electrical power $p_\text{in}=V_S i$ crosses into
the boundary. Inside, the resistor converts electrical energy to internal
thermal energy at $i^2R$, while the capacitor accumulates field energy. KVL gives
the exact lumped balance

$$
V_S i=i^2R+v_C i
=i^2R+\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm dt}
  \left(\frac12 Cv_C^2\right).
$$ {#eq-f07-rc-power-balance}

During warm-up, $i^2R$ is an internal conversion; heat has not necessarily
crossed the outer physical boundary yet. Do not count the same energy once as
resistor dissipation and again as outward heat before it actually leaves.

For an initially uncharged ideal capacitor charged to $V_S$ through a resistor
by an ideal voltage step, $i=C\,\mathrm dv_C/\mathrm dt$ converts the source
integral into an integral over capacitor voltage:

$$
W_\text{source}
=\int_0^\infty V_S i\,\mathrm dt
=V_S\int_0^{V_S}C\,\mathrm dv_C
=CV_S^2.
$$

The capacitor retains $W_C=\tfrac12CV_S^2$. Integrating
@eq-f07-rc-power-balance, or subtracting the final stored energy from the only
cross-boundary input, gives
$W_R=W_\text{source}-W_C=\tfrac12CV_S^2$. Therefore

$$
W_\text{source}=CV_S^2,
\qquad
W_C(\infty)=\frac12CV_S^2,
\qquad
W_R=\frac12CV_S^2.
$$ {#eq-f07-rc-charge-energy}

The 50% split is exact only for this ideal step, zero initial voltage, constant
source voltage, and resistive path. Changing $R$ changes how quickly energy is
converted but not this integrated split. A shaped current source, energy-recovery
circuit, nonzero initial voltage, leakage path, or non-ideal source changes it.

For the 5.00 V, 10.0 $\mu$F example,

$$
W_C=\frac12(10.0~\mu\text{F})(5.00~\text{V})^2
=125~\mu\text{J}.
$$

The source delivers 250 $\mu$J and the resistor converts 125 $\mu$J. This is a
calculation under declared assumptions, not a measured energy budget.

For a source-free RL loop, choose a boundary enclosing $L$ and $R$. No electrical
power crosses the boundary after disconnection. Stored magnetic energy decreases
while resistor internal energy increases:

$$
0=i^2R+\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm dt}
  \left(\frac12Li^2\right).
$$ {#eq-f07-rl-power-balance}

Integrating over $0\le t<\infty$, as $i(t)\to0$, shows that the resistor and any
other declared clamp elements together receive the initial $\tfrac12LI_0^2$. If an arc,
switch avalanche, winding capacitance, or radiation takes part, those are
additional paths to inventory rather than unexplained “lost energy.”

## Switching and flyback protection

In @fig-f07-flyback, $i_L$ is referenced downward through a coil with winding
resistance $R_w$. While switch $S$ is closed, flyback diode $D_F$ is reverse
biased. When $S$ opens, inductor-current continuity drives TP1 upward until the
diode conducts from TP1 back toward the supply. The current then has a closed,
controlled loop through $R_w$, $L$, and $D_F$.

![A low-side switched inductive load with a flyback diode across the winding.](../../../build/circuits/f07-flyback-clamp.png){#fig-f07-flyback width="55%" fig-alt="Source V_S feeds winding resistance R_w and inductor L, followed by low-side switch S to TP0. Diode D_F is connected from switch node TP1 upward to the winding supply side. It is reverse biased while S is closed and carries the retained inductor current upward after S opens."}

### Illustrative turn-off calculation

Use **illustrative**, not component-specific, values
$V_S=12.0$ V DC, $L=100$ mH, $R_w=120~\Omega$, and steady current
$I_0=100$ mA immediately before opening. The stored energy is

$$
W_L(0)=\frac12(0.100~\text{H})(0.100~\text{A})^2
=0.500~\text{mJ}.
$$

Energy can be small while voltage is large. For a source-free screening estimate,
suppose an unclamped, lossless first description transfers all magnetic energy
into only 100 pF of parasitic capacitance. Neglecting source work is reasonable
only as an order-of-magnitude screen when the estimated excursion is far above
the 12 V supply. Then

$$
\frac12LI_0^2=\frac12C_pV_p^2
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
V_p=I_0\sqrt{\frac{L}{C_p}}
\approx3.16~\text{kV}.
$$

This is not a prediction that TP1 will reach 3.16 kV. Breakdown, arcing, switch
avalanche, distributed capacitance, resistance, and electromagnetic emission
may intervene first. It is an order-of-magnitude warning that a 12 V supply does
not bound the turn-off voltage.

Approximate the conducting diode by constant forward drop $V_F=0.70$ V. In the
flyback loop,

$$
L\frac{\mathrm di}{\mathrm dt}+R_wi+V_F=0.
$$ {#eq-f07-flyback-ode}

While the diode conducts and $0<i\le I_0$, the solution is

$$
i(t)=\left(I_0+\frac{V_F}{R_w}\right)e^{-R_wt/L}
-\frac{V_F}{R_w}.
$$

The constant-drop description ends when this expression reaches zero; extending
it further would predict an unphysical negative diode current.

At the instant after opening,
$v_L(0^+)=-(R_wI_0+V_F)=-12.7$ V and TP1 is clamped near
$V_S+V_F=12.7$ V, ignoring wiring inductance and diode dynamics. Setting the
conducting-interval expression to zero gives

$$
t_0=\frac{L}{R_w}
\ln\!\left(1+\frac{R_wI_0}{V_F}\right)
=2.42~\text{ms}.
$$

A higher-voltage TVS or Zener clamp makes $|\mathrm di/\mathrm dt|$ larger and
releases the actuator faster, but raises switch stress. A simple diode reduces
voltage stress but can delay relay or solenoid release. Protection selection must
therefore check maximum supply, switch rating with margin, peak and repetitive
clamp current, stored energy, diode reverse voltage and recovery, temperature,
and the required mechanical release time [@horowitz2015art].

### Worked selection: release time versus switch stress

Continue the illustrative coil calculation, but add design requirements: the
current must reach zero within 1.00 ms; TP1 must remain strictly below 28.0 V for
$V_S\le12.0$ V; the initial current is at most 100 mA; repetition is at most
100 operations/s; and the ambient range is within the eventual component
datasheets. Treat clamp voltages in this comparison as illustrative constants,
not guaranteed device values.

| Turn-off path | Clamp above $V_S$ | Calculated $t_0$ | TP1 screen | Decision |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| silicon flyback diode | 0.70 V | 2.42 ms | 12.7 V | voltage passes; release fails |
| diode + 12.0 V Zener | 12.7 V | 0.554 ms | 24.7 V | both screens pass |
| TVS target near 13 V | about 13 V | about 0.54 ms | about 25 V | feasible; tolerance must be checked |

The diode-plus-12 V-Zener path is the bounded **concept selection** because it
meets both calculated screens with more TP1 margin than a target close to 16 V.
Its initial clamp power screen is
$(12.7~\text{V})(0.100~\text{A})=1.27$ W for a sub-millisecond pulse. The total
stored-energy rate at 100 operations/s is at most
$(0.500~\text{mJ})(100~\text{s}^{-1})=50.0$ mW before allocating energy between
$R_w$, diode, and Zener.

This is not yet a part acceptance. A real selection must use worst-case Zener or
TVS clamp voltage at 100 mA, tolerance, dynamic resistance, temperature, pulse
energy curve, average power, diode forward voltage and recovery, wiring
overshoot, and the switch's derated rating. The guarded hardware rule is
$v_{SW,\text{peak}}+U_V<28.0$ V at the maximum supply, current, repetition rate,
and temperature, together with measured current-decay time plus timing
uncertainty strictly below 1.00 ms. If either guarded inequality touches its
limit, the path fails.

::: {.callout-warning title="A clamp is part of the energy path"}
Do not add a diode by symbol recognition alone. Verify its orientation, the loop
that exists after opening, and every element that absorbs energy. A reversed
diode may short the supply when the switch closes; a missing connection may leave
the inductor unclamped; and a diode adequate for one isolated pulse may overheat
under repetition.
:::

## Non-ideal behavior and validity limits

Real capacitors and inductors are multi-parameter components. A useful first
description adds only the effect needed to explain the observation, while keeping
the omitted effects visible.

| Effect | First added element or relation | Observable consequence | Design implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| capacitor leakage | parallel $R_\text{leak}$ | lower loaded DC final voltage when source resistance is finite, or self-discharge | final value and discharge time change |
| capacitor ESR | series $R_\text{ESR}$ | terminal step $\Delta v=R_\text{ESR}\Delta i$ when current steps, and ripple heating | pulse current and temperature are limited |
| capacitor ESL | series $L_\text{ESL}$ | impedance stops falling and becomes inductive above self-resonance | placement and package geometry matter |
| nonlinear dielectric | $q=q(v,T,\ldots)$ and differential $C_d=\mathrm dq/\mathrm dv$ | time constant changes with bias and temperature | nominal marking is insufficient |
| inductor winding resistance | series $R_w$ | lower final current and copper heating | include DCR in $R_\text{th}$ and power |
| core saturation | nonlinear $\lambda(i)$; incremental $L$ falls | current rises faster than constant-$L$ prediction | current limit must precede saturation damage |
| core loss | frequency- and waveform-dependent loss | additional heating and phase change | DC copper loss alone is incomplete |
| inter-turn or self-capacitance | parallel parasitic $C_p$ | self-resonance and ringing | inductor can look capacitive at high frequency |
| mutual coupling | flux linkage depends on other winding currents | induced voltages and changed equivalent $L$ | orientation and coupling coefficient matter |

Manufacturer documentation confirms that aluminum-capacitor leakage depends on
voltage, temperature, time after energization, and storage history; its stated
acceptance condition can be tied to a specified time such as five minutes at
20 °C rather than being one timeless resistance [@vishay2026aluminumcaps]. The
same source treats ESR, ESL, ripple-current heating, and frequency as separate
conditions. Murata's measured capacitor impedance examples show a minimum at
self-resonance and inductive behavior above it, so an ideal $1/(2\pi fC)$ trend
cannot be extended indefinitely [@murataemifilter6].

For power inductors, “rated current” may be defined by temperature rise, by an
inductance-drop criterion, or by a manufacturer's stated test setup. PCB copper,
airflow, ambient temperature, waveform, and neighboring heat sources alter the
application result. Würth's application note documents these dependencies and
uses separate rated and saturation-current concepts; values from different test
conditions are not automatically comparable [@blakey2025ratedcurrent].

### Instrument loading and bandwidth

An oscilloscope probe connected from TP1 to TP0 adds input resistance in parallel
with the discharge path and input capacitance in parallel with the circuit
capacitance. A nominal $R$ and $C$ therefore become, to first order,

$$
R_\text{effective}=R\parallel R_\text{probe},
\qquad
C_\text{effective}=C+C_\text{probe}.
$$

The probe may make a high-resistance discharge appear faster even while its
capacitance slows a low-capacitance edge. Probe ground-lead inductance can add
ringing that is not present at the intended test point. Record the probe mode,
input resistance and capacitance, bandwidth limit, coupling, sample rate,
trigger, ground connection, calibration state, and uncertainty basis. The
instrument indication belongs to the circuit-plus-instrument system, as F06
established.

## Executable cross-check and synthetic diagnosis

The following standard-library Python computes the complete first-order response.
It is **executable arithmetic**, not a field solver, circuit simulator, or
measurement. The parameter names match @eq-f07-first-order-solution.

```python
from math import exp


def first_order(time_s, initial, final, tau_s):
    if tau_s <= 0.0:
        raise ValueError("tau_s must be positive")
    distance = initial - final
    return final + distance * exp(-time_s / tau_s)


rc_tau_s = 10.0e3 * 10.0e-6
rl_tau_s = 0.100 / 100.0

for multiple in (0.0, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0):
    rc_v = first_order(
        multiple * rc_tau_s, 0.0, 5.0, rc_tau_s
    )
    rl_a = first_order(
        multiple * rl_tau_s, 0.0, 0.050, rl_tau_s
    )
    print(f"{multiple:3.0f} tau: {rc_v:5.3f} V, "
          f"{1e3 * rl_a:6.3f} mA")
```

Expected output, verified with Python 3:

```text
  0 tau: 0.000 V,  0.000 mA
  1 tau: 3.161 V, 31.606 mA
  3 tau: 4.751 V, 47.511 mA
  5 tau: 4.966 V, 49.663 mA
```

The limiting cases are explicit: at zero time the initial values are returned;
as the multiple grows, both approach but never equal their final values in
finite time. If changing $R$ in code makes both RC and RL slower, one of the time
constant assignments is wrong.

Each representation occupies a different evidence rung:

| Rung used here or downstream | What it can support | What it cannot yet support |
|---|---|---|
| order-of-magnitude estimate | expected timescale or stress scale | precise component behavior |
| analytic calculation | prediction under declared ideal assumptions | a physical assembly claim |
| executable arithmetic | check that the printed equation was implemented consistently | independent physics evidence |
| synthetic data | practice fitting and discriminating hypotheses | a measurement claim |
| L05 measurement | first comparison on one documented assembly | population or environmental qualification |
| repeated units and corners | bounded reproducibility under tested conditions | untested field conditions |

The evidence should stop at the lowest rung adequate for the decision. The code
above cross-checks arithmetic; the next example teaches diagnosis; neither
raises a physical claim to the measurement rung.

### Synthetic diagnosis: a charging curve stops low

Suppose the 5.00 V, 10.0 k$\Omega$, 10.0 $\mu$F RC circuit is expected to follow
the ideal example, but the following values appear. The “observed” column is
**synthetic**, generated from a hidden 100 k$\Omega$ leakage path and rounded to
10 mV. Assigning an illustrative $\pm0.02$ V observation uncertainty does not
turn it into measurement evidence; the table teaches diagnosis only.

| Time after switch | Ideal prediction | Synthetic observation |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 ms | 0.00 V | $0.00\pm0.02$ V |
| 50 ms | 1.97 V | $1.92\pm0.02$ V |
| 100 ms | 3.16 V | $3.03\pm0.02$ V |
| 200 ms | 4.32 V | $4.04\pm0.02$ V |
| 500 ms | 4.97 V | $4.53\pm0.02$ V |

The disagreement is structured: the curve is somewhat faster and its plateau is
low. @fig-f07-leaky makes the added path explicit: it loads the DC divider and
also appears in the resistance seen by $C$.

![A charging RC circuit with a leakage resistance in parallel with the capacitor.](../../../build/circuits/f07-leaky-rc.png){#fig-f07-leaky fig-alt="A 5 volt source drives TP1 through 10 kilohm resistor R. Capacitor C of 10 microfarads and leakage resistance R_leak both connect from TP1 to reference TP0."}

This leakage resistance from TP1 to TP0 predicts

$$
V_\infty
=V_S\frac{R_\text{leak}}{R+R_\text{leak}},
\qquad
\tau=(R\parallel R_\text{leak})C.
$$ {#eq-f07-leaky-rc}

Using an illustrative fitted asymptote $V_\infty\approx4.55$ V (distinct from
the rounded 500 ms sample),

$$
R_\text{leak}
=R\frac{V_\infty}{V_S-V_\infty}
\approx(10.0~\text{k}\Omega)
\frac{4.55}{5.00-4.55}
\approx101~\text{k}\Omega.
$$

The predicted effective time constant is then approximately
$(10.0~\text{k}\Omega\parallel101~\text{k}\Omega)(10.0~\mu\text{F})
=91.0$ ms, consistent with the synthetic curve. That agreement makes leakage a
plausible hypothesis, not a proven location. A contaminated board, oscilloscope
input, wrong resistor, source sag, or capacitor leakage can create the same
terminal behavior. Discriminating measurements would separately verify the
source terminals, measure the de-energized resistance seen from TP1, repeat with the
probe removed or changed, and inspect the component value and board condition.

## Worked design: a bounded capacitor discharge

@fig-f07-bleed keeps $R_\text{bleed}$ permanently across $C$, so opening $S$
removes the source without removing the intended discharge path. TP1 is the
measurand node used in the calculation and acceptance rule.

![A capacitor with a permanently connected bleed resistor and a switched charging source.](../../../build/circuits/f01-capacitor-bleed.png){#fig-f07-bleed fig-alt="Source V_S charges capacitor C through switch S. Bleed resistor R_bleed remains connected in parallel with C after S opens. TP1 marks capacitor voltage relative to the return."}

An **illustrative** extra-low-voltage assembly has these requirements and assumed
component bounds:

- capacitor voltage immediately after isolation:
  $0\le V_0\le12.6$ V DC;
- capacitance: $C=470~\mu$F with a declared worst-case bound of $\pm20\%$ over
  the applicable conditions, so $C_\text{max}=564~\mu$F;
- a permanently connected bleed resistor with $\pm5\%$ tolerance;
- unobserved capacitor voltage strictly below 1.00 V within 30.0 s after
  isolation; and
- initial resistor dissipation below 0.250 W.

These bounds are teaching inputs, not specifications of an unnamed product.
Temperature, aging, voltage coefficient, fault tolerance, creepage, and the
consequence of an open resistor would require a real datasheet and system hazard
analysis.

For source-free discharge,
$v_C(t)=V_0e^{-t/(RC)}$. The slowest case uses $V_{0,\max}$,
$C_\text{max}$, and $R_\text{max}$. The timing requirement gives

$$
R_\text{max}<
\frac{30.0~\text{s}}
{(564~\mu\text{F})\ln(12.6/1.00)}
\approx20.99~\text{k}\Omega.
$$ {#eq-f07-bleed-rmax}

Choose 18.0 k$\Omega$, $\pm5\%$, so
$R_\text{max}=18.9$ k$\Omega$. Its worst-case crossing time is

$$
t_{1\text{V},\max}
=(18.9~\text{k}\Omega)(564~\mu\text{F})
\ln(12.6/1.00)
=27.0~\text{s}.
$$

This leaves 3.0 s of calculated timing margin under the declared bounds. The
largest initial resistor power occurs at maximum voltage and minimum resistance,
$R_\text{min}=17.1$ k$\Omega$:

$$
P_{R,\max}(0)
=\frac{(12.6~\text{V})^2}{17.1~\text{k}\Omega}
=9.28~\text{mW}<0.250~\text{W}.
$$

The maximum initially stored energy is

$$
W_{C,\max}
=\frac12(564~\mu\text{F})(12.6~\text{V})^2
=44.8~\text{mJ}.
$$

The design therefore passes the **illustrative calculation**. It is not yet a
verified assembly.

### Acceptance test and decision rule

For a physical low-energy implementation under an approved procedure, the
measurand is $V_C=V_{\mathrm{TP1}}-V_{\mathrm{TP0}}$ at 30.0 s after isolation.
Record actual $V_0$, ambient temperature, fitted component identities and values,
instrument input resistance/capacitance, range, bandwidth, calibration status,
timing uncertainty, raw waveform, and the coverage basis and factor for a
one-sided expanded contribution $U_V$. Here $U_V$ must include instrument
accuracy/calibration inputs and the voltage effect of sample-time uncertainty.
A 10 M$\Omega$ probe in parallel with 18 k$\Omega$ reduces the discharge
resistance by about 0.18%; that loading must be bounded because it can make a
failing discharge appear to pass. Let $\Delta V_\text{load,max}\ge0$ bound the
unobserved 30 s voltage minus the loaded indication under the declared component
limits. With exactly 10 M$\Omega$, 18.9 k$\Omega$, 564 $\mu$F, and 12.6 V, the
first-order screen gives 0.755 V unloaded and 0.751 V loaded, so the directional
loading allowance is about 4 mV. A real test must use the guaranteed probe input
bound rather than the nominal 10 M$\Omega$ marking.

Use the guarded rule

$$
\boxed{\text{pass only if }V_\text{indicated}(30.0~\text{s})
+U_V+\Delta V_\text{load,max}<1.00~\text{V}.}
$$ {#eq-f07-bleed-decision}

Equality does not satisfy the strict requirement. Also apply a one-sided guarded
rule to the initial-voltage limit—its indicated value plus the relevant expanded
uncertainty must not exceed 12.6 V—and verify by an approved method that the
resistance path remains present after power removal. A single pass supports only
the tested unit and conditions; repeated units, environmental corners, aging,
and open-circuit fault coverage require higher evidence rungs
[@jcgm2012conformity].

## Summary

A capacitor's charge separation is represented one-to-one by voltage in the
linear range because $q=Cv_C$ and $i_C=C\,\mathrm dv_C/\mathrm dt$. An
inductor's flux linkage is represented one-to-one by current because
$\lambda=Li_L$ and
$v_L=L\,\mathrm di_L/\mathrm dt$. Under finite excitation, capacitor voltage and
inductor current are continuous. Those relations generate the first-order
pattern

$$
x(t)=x_\infty+[x(0^+)-x_\infty]e^{-t/\tau},
$$

with $\tau=R_\text{th}C$ for RC and $\tau=L/R_\text{th}$ for RL. Initial value,
final value, time constant, and a declared reference are enough only when the
post-switch network is linear, time invariant, and genuinely first order.

Energy explains what the exponential alone cannot: a charging capacitor
accumulates electric-field energy while resistance converts part of the input;
an inductor whose current is interrupted must send magnetic-field energy into a
resistor, clamp, arc, avalanche path, or parasitic field. Leakage, ESR, DCR,
saturation, self-resonance, and instrument loading are not afterthoughts; they
define where the first-order description stops agreeing with the physical
circuit.

## Exercises

### Quick check

Choose one best answer for each item.

1. An ideal 10 $\mu$F capacitor has $v_C(0^-)=3.0$ V. A switch changes a
   finite-resistance network at $t=0$. What is $v_C(0^+)$?

    a. 0 V;
    b. 3.0 V;
    c. the new source voltage;
    d. impossible to state because capacitor voltage always jumps.

2. A first-order rising response has elapsed for $5\tau$. Approximately what
   fraction of the total change is complete?

    a. 50%;
    b. 63.2%;
    c. 99.3%;
    d. exactly 100%.

3. A source-free RL loop has $i_L(0^+)>0$ under passive-sign references. What is
   the sign of $v_L(0^+)$ while current decays?

    a. positive;
    b. negative;
    c. zero for every inductor;
    d. undefined.

4. Doubling $R_\text{th}$ while holding the storage element fixed has which
   effect on the time constants?

    a. RC and RL both become twice as slow;
    b. RC and RL both become twice as fast;
    c. RC time constant doubles; RL time constant halves;
    d. RC time constant halves; RL time constant doubles.

5. Why is a flyback diode placed across a DC relay coil driven by a low-side
   switch?

    a. to force inductor current to zero instantaneously;
    b. to increase the steady coil current without limit;
    c. to provide a controlled path for retained current after switch opening;
    d. to make the coil purely resistive at every frequency.

6. A nominal 5 V RC charge settles near 4.5 V and also reaches its plateau sooner
   than predicted. Which single added effect explains both observations?

    a. a leakage resistance from the capacitor node to return;
    b. a larger ideal capacitance only;
    c. an open series resistor;
    d. infinite oscilloscope input resistance.

**Answer key:** 1 b — finite current preserves capacitor voltage; 2 c — the
remaining error is $e^{-5}$; 3 b — the inductor delivers energy; 4 c — compare
$RC$ with $L/R$; 5 c — current continuity needs a loop; 6 a — leakage lowers the
divider final value and reduces the resistance seen by $C$.

### Retrieval and explanation

1. State the passive-sign references required before writing
   $i_C=C\,\mathrm dv_C/\mathrm dt$.
2. State the winding, flux, voltage, and current orientation needed before
   writing $v_L=\mathrm d\lambda/\mathrm dt$.
3. Distinguish natural, forced, complete, zero-input, and zero-state responses.
4. Explain why “a capacitor is an open circuit” is conditional rather than
   universal.
5. Explain why a small stored energy does not guarantee a small switch-opening
   voltage.
6. Name four records needed to make an oscilloscope transient reproducible.
7. State the conditions under which series capacitors and parallel inductors may
   be replaced by the simple equivalents in the chapter table. Give one initial
   charge/current or coupling condition that invalidates a naive reduction.

### Estimation and calculation

1. Estimate and then calculate the time constant of 47 k$\Omega$ and 2.2
   $\mu$F. How long is required to enter a 1% final-value band?
   **Self-check:** $\tau=0.103$ s and $t_{1\%}=0.476$ s.
2. A 220 $\mu$F capacitor is initially at 9.0 V and discharges through 33 k$\Omega$.
   Calculate $v_C$ at 5.0 s and the initial stored energy.
3. A 24 V source drives a 240 $\Omega$ winding with $L=480$ mH. Starting from
   zero current, find $i_L(\tau)$, $v_L(\tau)$, and stored energy at steady DC.
4. An inductor carries 2.0 A and stores 8.0 mJ in its constant-$L$ range. Find
   $L$. If 20 V is applied with the passive polarity, estimate the initial
   current slope. **Self-check:** $L=4.0$ mH and
   $\mathrm di/\mathrm dt=5.0$ kA/s.
5. A capacitor has $v_C(0^-)=2.00$ V. At $t=0$ it is connected to a 12.0 V
   divider with 3.00 k$\Omega$ from the source to TP1, 6.00 k$\Omega$ from TP1
   to return, and $C=20.0~\mu$F from TP1 to return. Find $V_0$, $V_\infty$,
   $R_\text{th}$, $\tau$, and the signed initial slope; then sketch and label
   $0^-$, $0^+$, $1\tau$, and the final value. **Self-check:**
   $V_\infty=8.00$ V, $R_\text{th}=2.00$ k$\Omega$,
   $\tau=40.0$ ms, and the initial slope is $+150$ V/s.

### Derivation and limiting cases

1. Starting from KCL and $i_C=C\,\mathrm dv_C/\mathrm dt$, derive the response in @eq-f07-rc-complete for
   arbitrary $V_0$ and constant $V_\text{th}$.
2. Starting from KVL and $v_L=L\,\mathrm di_L/\mathrm dt$, derive the response in @eq-f07-rl-complete. Verify
   its dimensions and both endpoints.
3. Integrate the balance in @eq-f07-rc-power-balance to derive the energy split
   in @eq-f07-rc-charge-energy. State every assumption that makes the split
   independent of $R$.
4. Take the limits $R\to0$ and $R\to\infty$ separately for RC and RL. Identify
   which limiting circuits cease to have the finite exponential final value
   assumed in the derivation.
5. Derive @eq-f07-flyback-ode's time-to-zero expression for constant $V_F$.
   Compare the limit $V_F\to0$ with ordinary exponential RL decay.

### Data interpretation

1. Refit the synthetic table in this chapter using the last value as the final
   plateau. Estimate $R_\text{leak}$ and $C$. List two alternate physical causes
   that the terminal curve alone cannot distinguish.
2. A synthetic RL current record is 0, 28.3, 40.6, 48.2, and 49.9 mA at 0, 1,
   2, 4, and 8 ms. The intended final current is 50.0 mA. Estimate $\tau$ from at
   least two points. Decide whether $L=100$ mH with $R=100~\Omega$ is consistent.
   State why the conclusion remains synthetic.
3. A scope trace rings immediately after a flyback switch opens. Construct a
   comparison table for three hypotheses: probe-ground inductance, inadequate
   clamp placement, and diode forward-recovery or junction-capacitance effects.
   For each, specify one change that should alter the trace if the hypothesis is
   correct.
4. A proposed RC waveform jumps from 2 V at $0^-$ to 5 V at $0^+$ even though
   every branch current is bounded, then initially slopes away from its stated
   4 V final value. Identify both contradictions, redraw a qualitatively possible
   waveform, and name an impulse or topology change that could make either
   observation physically credible.

### Debugging

1. A capacitor discharge appears twice as fast when a 1 M$\Omega$ probe is
   connected. The intended bleed is 1 M$\Omega$. Diagnose the loading
   quantitatively and propose a less intrusive measurement.
2. An RL calculation predicts 100 mA steady current, but the measured value is
   82 mA. List and order checks for source sag, winding DCR, added series
   resistance, heating, current-instrument burden, and saturation. Which checks
   can explain a lower final DC current without changing $L$?
3. A relay releases too slowly after a flyback diode is fitted, but the switch
   is now safely below its voltage rating. Explain the trade-off and propose two
   higher-voltage clamp approaches. Name the new ratings that must be verified.

### Open design

1. Design a bleed path for a 24 V, 1000 $\mu$F capacitor that must fall below
   2.0 V in 20 s. Define real component bounds, resistor pulse and steady-power
   requirements, meter loading, an uncertainty budget, an open-resistor fault
   response, and a guarded acceptance rule.
2. Design the turn-off protection for an illustrative 24 V solenoid with known
   $L$, DCR, maximum current, repetition rate, and required release time. Compare
   a diode, diode-plus-Zener, and TVS. Trace stored energy into named components
   and state the evidence needed before accepting the design.
3. Create a current-limited, extra-low-voltage plan for distinguishing capacitor
   leakage from oscilloscope loading. Include schematic test points, predicted
   curves, component tolerances, instrument settings, raw-data fields, stop
   conditions, and the claim permitted by one run versus repeated units.

## Connections

Chapter ID: `F07`.

- **Prerequisites:** [F04 — Network conservation and equivalent circuits](f04-networks-kirchhoff.qmd)
  supplies KCL, KVL, and Thévenin resistance; [F05 — Energy, power, efficiency,
  and heat](f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd) supplies passive-sign power and boundary
  balances; [M02 — Calculus, linear algebra, and differential equations](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd)
  supplies the derivative, integral, and first-order solution.
- **Next on the dynamics spine:** [F08 — Sinusoids, impedance, and frequency
  response](f08-sinusoids-impedance-frequency.qmd) turns the same $C$ and $L$
  relations into frequency-dependent impedance, resonance, and filters.
- **Downstream use:** [D03 — State, clocks, and timing](../03-digital/d03-state-clock-timing.qmd)
  uses settling and stored state; [S04 — Feedback and control](../05-domains/s04-feedback-control.qmd)
  uses time constants, poles, and transient performance; power conversion and
  actuator chapters inherit flyback-energy reasoning.
- **Practice:** Lab **L05** supplies physical RC/RL transient and frequency
  evidence under the Part I safety boundary. The [practice and project
  spine](../roadmap.qmd#practice-and-project-spine) maps F07 into milestone **M2**.
- **Just-in-time support:** [F06 — Measurement, uncertainty, and debugging](f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd)
  supports loading and guarded decisions; [M01 — Algebra, units, and complex
  numbers](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd) supports SI and equation
  manipulation.

## References

Point-of-use citations above identify the governing circuit and field treatments
[@sedra2020microelectronic; @griffiths2017electrodynamics;
@ulaby2015electromagnetics], signals terminology [@oppenheim1996signals], guarded
decision guidance [@jcgm2012conformity], safety boundary [@iec61010], and
manufacturer-primary documents used for capacitor and inductor limits
[@vishay2026aluminumcaps; @murataemifilter6; @blakey2025ratedcurrent]. The
complete [bibliography](../references.qmd) contains full details. These sources
do not replace local authorization or physical qualification of an assembly.
